name: The Holy Trinity
description: The belief that God is one being in three co-equal, co-eternal persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

positions.position: One God in three consubstantial persons. Defined at Nicaea (325) and Constantinople (381). Includes the Filioque — the Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father and the Son.
positions.scoreJustification: Trinitarian belief is attested from apostolic times in baptismal formulas and early creeds. The Nicene definition articulated what was already believed.

sources.label: Ignatius of Antioch, Letter to the Ephesians
Nicene Creed (325 AD)

positions.position: One God in three consubstantial persons. Rejects the Filioque — the Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father alone through the Son.
positions.scoreJustification: Holds the same Trinitarian theology as the early church. The Filioque dispute is about the internal procession, not the Trinity itself.

sources.label: Nicene Creed (325 AD)

positions.position: One God in three consubstantial persons. Follows the original Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed without the Filioque.
positions.scoreJustification: Full Trinitarian orthodoxy consistent with early church.

sources.label: Nicene Creed (325 AD)

positions.position: Affirms the Nicene and Athanasian Creeds. One God in three persons, including the Filioque.
positions.scoreJustification: Retained full Trinitarian orthodoxy from Catholic tradition.

positions.position: Affirms classical Trinitarian theology as expressed in the ecumenical creeds.
positions.scoreJustification: Retained full Trinitarian orthodoxy.

positions.position: Affirms the Nicene Creed and classical Trinitarianism. Included in the Thirty-Nine Articles.
positions.scoreJustification: Retained full Trinitarian orthodoxy.

positions.position: Affirms the Trinity. God eternally exists in three persons.
positions.scoreJustification: Retained full Trinitarian orthodoxy.

positions.position: Affirms classical Trinitarian theology as in the Articles of Religion.
positions.scoreJustification: Retained full Trinitarian orthodoxy.

positions.position: Affirms the Trinity, though with less emphasis on creedal formulations.
positions.scoreJustification: Retained full Trinitarian orthodoxy despite de-emphasizing creeds.

positions.position: Most Pentecostal denominations affirm the Trinity. Oneness Pentecostals hold a modalist view (one God manifesting in three modes).
positions.scoreJustification: Mainstream Pentecostals are Trinitarian, but the significant Oneness movement within Pentecostalism departs from early church Trinitarianism.

name: The Eucharist / Lord's Supper
description: Beliefs about the nature of communion — whether Christ is truly present in the bread and wine, and in what manner.

positions.position: Real Presence through transubstantiation — the substance of bread and wine truly becomes the Body and Blood of Christ while the appearances remain.
positions.scoreJustification: The earliest Church Fathers (Ignatius, Justin Martyr, Irenaeus) consistently taught the Real Presence. Transubstantiation articulated the mechanism of a belief held from the beginning.

sources.label: Ignatius of Antioch, Letter to the Smyrnaeans (c. 110 AD)
Justin Martyr, First Apology, ch. 66 (c. 155 AD)
Irenaeus, Against Heresies, Book IV, ch. 18 (c. 180 AD)

positions.position: Real Presence — the bread and wine truly become the Body and Blood of Christ. The mechanism is a divine mystery, not defined as precisely as transubstantiation.
positions.scoreJustification: Consistent Real Presence belief from the early church, differing from Catholicism only in the philosophical explanation of the mechanism.

sources.label: John Chrysostom, Homily on Matthew (c. 390 AD)

positions.position: Real Presence — the elements truly become the Body and Blood of Christ through the Holy Spirit's action in the epiclesis.
positions.scoreJustification: Consistent Real Presence belief from the early church.

sources.label: Cyril of Alexandria, Commentary on John (c. 430 AD)

positions.position: Real Presence through sacramental union — Christ's body and blood are truly present 'in, with, and under' the bread and wine. Rejects transubstantiation but affirms Real Presence.
positions.scoreJustification: Affirms the Real Presence (consistent with early church) but rejects the traditional explanation of how it occurs.

positions.position: Spiritual presence — Christ is spiritually present in communion but not physically. The faithful receive Christ by faith through the Holy Spirit.
positions.scoreJustification: Maintains a form of Christ's presence but rejects the physical Real Presence taught by early Church Fathers.

positions.position: Range of views from Real Presence to receptionism. The Thirty-Nine Articles reject transubstantiation but affirm Christ's presence in a manner not precisely defined.
positions.scoreJustification: Broad range of belief; many Anglicans affirm Real Presence, but the official position is deliberately ambiguous.

positions.position: Memorial/ordinance view — communion is a symbolic remembrance of Christ's death. The bread and grape juice are symbols, not the literal body and blood.
positions.scoreJustification: Explicitly rejects the Real Presence taught by all early Church Fathers. A purely symbolic view has no attestation in the pre-Nicene period.

positions.position: Real spiritual presence — Christ is truly present in the sacrament through the Holy Spirit. Not a mere symbol, but not transubstantiation.
positions.scoreJustification: Affirms a real but non-physical presence, partially consistent with early church teaching.

positions.position: Memorial view — communion is an act of remembrance and community, symbolizing Christ's sacrifice. Not a sacrament but an ordinance.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects Real Presence and sacramental nature of the Eucharist, inconsistent with early church teaching.

positions.position: Typically memorial/symbolic view. Communion is an ordinance of remembrance rather than a sacrament conveying grace.
positions.scoreJustification: Symbolic view inconsistent with early church Real Presence teaching.

name: Justification / Salvation
description: How a person is made right with God — by faith alone, by works, or by a combination of faith and works cooperating with grace.

positions.position: Justification is by grace through faith, but faith must be alive and active in charity (works). Initial justification is received at baptism; ongoing justification involves cooperation with grace through the sacraments and good works.
positions.scoreJustification: Early Church Fathers consistently taught that faith and works/charity are both necessary. The Council of Trent articulated what the Fathers already held.

sources.label: Clement of Rome, First Epistle to the Corinthians, ch. 30-32 (c. 96 AD)
Justin Martyr, First Apology (c. 155 AD)

positions.position: Theosis (divinization) — salvation is a process of becoming partakers of the divine nature through cooperation with God's grace. Faith and works are inseparable aspects of the life in Christ.
positions.scoreJustification: Theosis is deeply rooted in early Church Fathers (Irenaeus, Athanasius). The synergistic view of faith and works matches early church teaching.

sources.label: Irenaeus, Against Heresies, Book V, Preface (c. 180 AD)

positions.position: Salvation through faith expressed in works and participation in the sacramental life of the Church. Similar to Eastern Orthodox theosis tradition.
positions.scoreJustification: Consistent with early church synergistic soteriology.

positions.position: Sola fide — justification by faith alone. Good works are the fruit of faith but do not contribute to justification. Forensic justification: God declares the sinner righteous through faith in Christ.
positions.scoreJustification: Sola fide as a formal principle is absent from pre-Nicene writings. Early Fathers consistently linked faith with works as necessary for salvation.

positions.position: Sola fide — justification by faith alone through grace alone. Irresistible grace and unconditional election. Good works are evidence of election, not a cause of justification.
positions.scoreJustification: Sola fide and irresistible grace are not attested in pre-Nicene sources.

positions.position: Justification by faith, with a range of emphases. The Thirty-Nine Articles lean Protestant (faith alone), but Anglo-Catholic tradition maintains a more Catholic view.
positions.scoreJustification: Mixed: official articles lean toward sola fide, but broad Anglican tradition includes views closer to the early church.

positions.position: Sola fide — salvation by grace through faith alone. Once genuinely saved, always saved (eternal security / perseverance of the saints).
positions.scoreJustification: Sola fide and eternal security are not attested in pre-Nicene writings.

positions.position: Justification by faith through prevenient grace. Wesley taught that grace enables free response, and believers can fall from grace. Sanctification is an ongoing process.
positions.scoreJustification: Wesley's synergism and possibility of falling from grace are closer to early church teaching than strict sola fide, but still frames justification primarily through faith.

positions.position: Faith expressed through discipleship and obedience. Salvation involves following Christ in daily life. Emphasis on visible fruit of repentance.
positions.scoreJustification: Emphasis on faith-and-works discipleship is somewhat consistent with early church, though sacramental framework is absent.

positions.position: Salvation by faith in Christ, evidenced by baptism in the Holy Spirit (often with speaking in tongues). Some hold eternal security, others believe in possibility of apostasy.
positions.scoreJustification: The distinctive emphasis on tongues as evidence of Spirit baptism is a novel development not found in early church soteriology.

name: Baptism
description: The nature, mode, and recipients of baptism — whether it confers grace, whether infants should be baptized, and what mode (immersion, pouring, sprinkling) is valid.

positions.position: Sacrament of regeneration that removes original sin and confers sanctifying grace. Infant baptism is normative. Valid by immersion, pouring, or sprinkling in the name of the Trinity.
positions.scoreJustification: Infant baptism and baptismal regeneration are attested in the earliest Church Fathers and the Didache. The early church consistently taught baptism as necessary for salvation.

sources.label: Didache, ch. 7 (c. 70-100 AD)
Justin Martyr, First Apology, ch. 61 (c. 155 AD)

positions.position: Sacrament (mystery) of regeneration. Infant baptism by triple immersion is normative, followed immediately by chrismation and first communion.
positions.scoreJustification: Consistent with early church practice of baptismal regeneration and infant baptism.

positions.position: Sacrament of regeneration. Infant baptism by immersion, followed by chrismation. Consistent with ancient practice.
positions.scoreJustification: Consistent with early church practice.

positions.position: Sacrament that effects regeneration and forgiveness. Infant baptism affirmed. Baptism is God's work, not the believer's decision.
positions.scoreJustification: Retained baptismal regeneration and infant baptism from Catholic tradition, consistent with early church.

positions.position: Sacrament and sign of the covenant. Infant baptism practiced as the New Covenant parallel to circumcision. Does not regenerate but is a means of grace.
positions.scoreJustification: Retains infant baptism but denies baptismal regeneration, which was taught by early Church Fathers.

positions.position: Sacrament of new birth and incorporation into Christ. Infant baptism normative. The Book of Common Prayer speaks of baptismal regeneration.
positions.scoreJustification: Retains baptismal regeneration and infant baptism consistent with early church.

positions.position: Ordinance (not sacrament) of believer's baptism only. Baptism is by full immersion following a personal profession of faith. Does not confer grace or regeneration.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects infant baptism and baptismal regeneration, both of which are well-attested in early church sources.

positions.position: Sacrament of initiation and prevenient grace. Infant baptism practiced. Baptism is a sign of regeneration and new birth.
positions.scoreJustification: Retains infant baptism and baptismal regeneration consistent with early church.

positions.position: Believer's baptism only. Baptism follows a conscious decision to follow Christ. Infant baptism is rejected as invalid. Baptism is an outward sign, not regenerative.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects infant baptism and baptismal regeneration, both attested in the early church.

positions.position: Believer's baptism by immersion. An ordinance of obedience following conversion. Not regenerative. Some practice re-baptism in Jesus' name only.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects infant baptism and baptismal regeneration. Jesus-name-only baptism contradicts the Trinitarian formula attested in the Didache.

name: Papal Authority
description: Whether the Bishop of Rome holds supreme authority over the universal Church, and if so, the nature and extent of that authority.

positions.position: The Pope, as successor of Saint Peter, holds supreme, full, and universal authority over the whole Church. Papal infallibility when speaking ex cathedra on faith and morals (defined 1870).
positions.scoreJustification: Roman primacy is attested by Clement of Rome (96 AD), Ignatius, and Irenaeus. The scope of authority developed over time, but the principle of Roman leadership is early.

sources.label: Clement of Rome, First Epistle to the Corinthians (c. 96 AD)
Irenaeus, Against Heresies, Book III, ch. 3 (c. 180 AD)

positions.position: The Bishop of Rome held a primacy of honor among equals (primus inter pares) but not supreme jurisdiction. Rejects papal infallibility. Church governance is conciliar.
positions.scoreJustification: Early church evidence shows both Roman primacy and conciliar governance. The Orthodox position reflects one strand of early practice.

positions.position: Rejects papal supremacy. Each church is autocephalous under its own patriarch. Honors the ancient patriarchates but recognizes no single supreme head.
positions.scoreJustification: Early church had multiple centers of authority; the degree of Roman supremacy in the pre-Nicene era is debated.

positions.position: Rejects papal authority entirely. The Pope may be a useful historical office but holds no divine authority over the Church.
positions.scoreJustification: Complete rejection of Roman primacy, which is attested in the earliest Church Fathers.

positions.position: Rejects papal authority. Church governance is presbyterian (rule by elders) rather than episcopal.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects both papal authority and the episcopal structure of the early church.

positions.position: Rejects papal jurisdiction. The monarch (or national church) is the supreme governor. Retains episcopal governance with archbishops.
positions.scoreJustification: Replaced papal authority with royal supremacy, a concept unknown to the early church.

positions.position: Rejects all hierarchical church authority. Each local congregation is autonomous and self-governing under Christ alone.
positions.scoreJustification: Congregational autonomy contradicts the hierarchical episcopal structure documented from the earliest church.

positions.position: No papal authority. Governed by a connexional system with bishops, but authority is shared through conferences.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects papal authority and replaces episcopal structure with a conference system.

positions.position: Rejects all external church authority. Each congregation is autonomous. No hierarchy beyond the local level.
positions.scoreJustification: Congregational autonomy contradicts early church hierarchical governance.

positions.position: Varies by denomination. Generally congregational or presbyterian governance. No recognition of papal authority.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects papal authority and early church episcopal structure.

name: Sola Scriptura / Sacred Tradition
description: Whether scripture alone is the sole rule of faith, or whether sacred tradition and the Church's teaching authority (Magisterium) are also authoritative sources of divine revelation.

positions.position: Scripture and Sacred Tradition together form the single deposit of faith, interpreted authoritatively by the Magisterium (teaching authority of the Church). Neither is sufficient alone.
positions.scoreJustification: Early Church Fathers consistently appealed to both scripture and apostolic tradition. Sola scriptura as a principle is absent from pre-Nicene sources.

sources.label: Irenaeus, Against Heresies III.3 — apostolic tradition through bishops (c. 180 AD)
Basil the Great, On the Holy Spirit, ch. 27 — unwritten traditions (c. 375 AD)

positions.position: Scripture is part of Holy Tradition, not separate from it. The Church, guided by the Holy Spirit through ecumenical councils, interprets both.
positions.scoreJustification: Consistent with the early church's understanding of scripture within the living tradition of the Church.

positions.position: Scripture and Tradition together form the basis of faith, interpreted by the Church through its councils and fathers.
positions.scoreJustification: Consistent with early church practice.

positions.position: Sola scriptura — Scripture alone is the sole infallible rule of faith. Tradition is valued but subordinate to and judged by scripture.
positions.scoreJustification: Sola scriptura as a formal principle is not found in the early church. The Fathers consistently appealed to tradition alongside scripture.

positions.position: Sola scriptura — The Bible alone is the Word of God and the only infallible rule for faith and practice.
positions.scoreJustification: Not attested in early church practice.

positions.position: Scripture contains all things necessary for salvation (prima scriptura). Tradition and reason are also authoritative (the 'three-legged stool' of Hooker).
positions.scoreJustification: The inclusion of tradition and reason alongside scripture is closer to the early church than strict sola scriptura.

positions.position: Sola scriptura — The Bible is the sole authority for faith and practice. No creed, tradition, or church body has binding authority.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects all extra-biblical authority, contrary to early church practice.

positions.position: The Wesleyan Quadrilateral — Scripture is primary, supplemented by tradition, reason, and experience.
positions.scoreJustification: The inclusion of tradition alongside scripture is partially consistent with early church, though 'experience' as an authority is novel.

positions.position: Sola scriptura with emphasis on the New Testament and the words of Christ. The community interprets scripture together.
positions.scoreJustification: Sola scriptura is not an early church principle.

positions.position: Sola scriptura, supplemented by ongoing personal revelation through the Holy Spirit (prophecy, words of knowledge).
positions.scoreJustification: Sola scriptura plus personal revelation as authority is doubly inconsistent with early church practice.

name: Purgatory
description: Whether there exists a state of purification after death for those who die in God's grace but are not yet fully purified.

positions.position: Purgatory exists as a state of purification after death for those who die in grace but with temporal punishment still due. Prayers for the dead are efficacious.
positions.scoreJustification: Prayers for the dead are attested in the earliest Christian catacombs and by early Fathers. The concept of post-death purification is ancient even if the term 'purgatory' is later.

sources.label: Clement of Alexandria, Stromata (c. 200 AD)
Augustine, City of God, Book XXI (c. 426 AD)

positions.position: Affirms an intermediate state and prayers for the dead, but rejects the Latin doctrine of purgatory as a place of punitive fire. The dead can benefit from the prayers of the living.
positions.scoreJustification: Affirms prayers for the dead (consistent with early church) but rejects the specific Western formulation of purgatory.

positions.position: Prayers for the dead are practiced. Rejects the Western doctrine of purgatory but acknowledges a state of waiting before final judgment.
positions.scoreJustification: Prayers for the dead are early church practice; rejection of purgatory as defined is a partial departure.

positions.position: Rejects purgatory entirely. Souls go directly to heaven or hell at death. Luther called purgatory a human invention unsupported by scripture.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects purgatory and prayers for the dead, both attested in the early church.

positions.position: Rejects purgatory. Souls go directly to be with Christ (heaven) or separated from God (hell).
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects post-death purification and prayers for the dead.

positions.position: The Thirty-Nine Articles call purgatory a 'Romish doctrine' to be rejected. Some Anglo-Catholics pray for the dead.
positions.scoreJustification: Officially rejects purgatory, though some Anglicans retain prayers for the dead.

positions.position: Rejects purgatory. Immediate judgment: heaven for believers, hell for unbelievers.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects purgatory and prayers for the dead.

positions.position: Rejects purgatory. Wesley did not teach an intermediate state of purification.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects purgatory and prayers for the dead.

positions.position: Rejects purgatory. Souls await the final resurrection and judgment.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects purgatory.

positions.position: Rejects purgatory. Immediate destiny after death: heaven or hell.
positions.scoreJustification: Rejects purgatory.
